narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and full-time commitment to studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 60% chance that you might not pass it initially, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower rankings in school grades—specifically, being in the lower deciles for both your first-year and third-year performance compared to typical students, who average much higher around the middle of their classes. Your third-year decile had the strongest negative pull, pushing the model firmly toward expecting a bar failure, while your first-year decile added to that concern. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, which is very close to the average of admitted students, worked strongly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success, and your undergraduate GPA, though below the typical 3.21 average, also helped a bit. Your middle family income bracket played a minor role in tipping slightly toward the risk side, but it wasn't a big factor. Overall, compared to students who typically get approved, your grade deciles stand out as below average, overshadowing your other solid qualities in the model's eyes. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many with similar starts have succeeded with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger study habits or coursework, perhaps retaking classes or seeking tutoring, and consider reapplying after demonstrating higher performance. You might also explore programs offering bar prep support or connect with mentors from similar backgrounds for guidance. We're rooting for you—law needs more voices like yours, and with some adjustments, you can strengthen your case next time. Keep pushing; your journey is just beginning.
